scriptPreview:
Getting into the network was only step one, and what Salt Typhoon did next is the reason US senators reached for words like "breathtaking" and "worst in history."
Inside the networks of AT&T, Verizon,
scriptFull:
Getting into the network was only step one, and what Salt Typhoon did next is the reason US senators reached for words like "breathtaking" and "worst in history."
Inside the networks of AT&T, Verizon, and Lumen Technologies, the group located the CALEA lawful intercept infrastructure — the dedicated portals, the surveillance systems, the backdoors that thirty years of federal law had required these companies to build, maintain, and leave accessible — and they walked through them without obstruction.
From that position, the access they obtained was comprehensive in a way that no ordinary network breach achieves.
They could access call metadata — who called whom, when, for how long, from where. For over a million users, concentrated heavily in the Washington D.C. metropolitan area. Senators, officials, defense contractors, intelligence community personnel, political campaign staff — the entire ecosystem of the American national security apparatus, mapped out in call records.
But call records were only half the picture. The metadata also included geolocation data — precise cell tower triangulation showing exactly where a device was at any given moment. Where a Senator slept. Where a defense contractor met a source. Where an intelligence officer traveled at two in the morning. Location data that granular does not just tell you what someone said. It tells you who they are, what they fear, and what they are trying to hide. In the business of counterintelligence, that is sometimes more valuable than the call itself.
They could access the content of unencrypted calls and text messages. Actual audio. Actual words. In a limited number of cases — fewer than one hundred individuals, according to the FBI — they recorded the actual content of phone calls. According to briefings provided to affected campaigns and confirmed by US investigators with high confidence, the targets included staff of the Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign, and phones belonging to Donald Trump and JD Vance.
And then there is the damage that most coverage failed to adequately convey.
By accessing the CALEA systems, Salt Typhoon could see the database of active wiretap requests — the full list of phone numbers currently under court-authorized surveillance by US law enforcement and intelligence agencies. That meant that if any of China's intelligence operatives inside the United States appeared on that list, Beijing would know in time to pull their people out, destroy evidence, and redirect operations before a single arrest could be made. The FBI could be closing in on a Chinese spy, building a counterintelligence case for months, preparing to act — and the target would already be gone. The entire US counterintelligence apparatus, built over decades at enormous cost, had effectively been handed to the adversary it was designed to protect against.
One former White House national security official described this to the Wall Street Journal as "a counterintelligence failure of the highest order," and that assessment is precise rather than hyperbolic. Salt Typhoon did not simply steal information — they compromised the machinery that protects information and turned the surveillance system against the people who built it.
